predictions.title: The "Agent Control Plane" becomes the new battleground in hyperscaler competition.
predictions.body: **The "Agent Control Plane" becomes the new battleground in hyperscaler competition.** Google's Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, AWS Bedrock AgentCore's new Managed Harness / CLI / skills, and OpenAI's Workspace Agents all converge on the same strategy: **bind the agent lifecycle, audit, and governance into a single control plane.** By Q3 2026, Okta / Microsoft Entra ID / Google Workspace SSO layers will treat agent identifiers as first-class objects alongside humans and service accounts. CASB / SSPM vendors will measure "API calls touched by AI agents" with a separate SLO / risk score from human-user baselines. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: Google Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, AWS Bedrock AgentCore, and OpenAI Workspace Agents land in the same window with one control-plane shape.
reasoning.given: Enterprises need a single place to govern agent lifecycle, audit, and identity as agents proliferate across SaaS and infrastructure.
reasoning.so_that: SSO providers treat agent IDs as first-class principals and CASB / SSPM vendors track AI-agent API calls under a separate risk score.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; Okta / Entra ID / Workspace agent-ID category + CASB / SSPM AI-agent metric live.
reasoning.plain_language: Three big clouds all built one place to manage AI agents this week. Soon login systems will treat agents like users with their own permissions.

predictions.summary: Google's Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, AWS Bedrock AgentCore, and OpenAI Workspace Agents converge on a single agent-lifecycle control plane. By Q3 2026, Okta / Entra ID / Google Workspace are expected to treat agent IDs as first-class principals while CASB / SSPM vendors carve out a separate risk metric for AI-agent API calls.

predictions.title: 27B Dense + 1M context" becomes the de facto standard for enterprise local LLMs in H2 2026.
predictions.body: **"27B Dense + 1M context" becomes the de facto standard for enterprise local LLMs in H2 2026.** Qwen3.6-27B demonstrates that the package — no MoE routing complexity, runs on a single RTX 4090, 1M context, Apache 2.0 — reaches flagship-grade coding ability. By Q3 2026, Mistral / Meta / DeepSeek / Google Gemma will each ship 20-30B Dense × 1M context × Apache/Llama-style models. The differentiators for 70B+ MoE narrow to multimodal understanding, tool breadth, and long-horizon reasoning. On-prem inference's default VRAM requirement shifts down from RTX 6000 48GB / H100 80GB to **RTX 4090 24GB / Apple M4 Max 64GB**. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: Qwen3.6-27B Dense beats the 397B MoE on SWE-bench Verified at 77.2% on a single RTX 4090 with 1M context under Apache 2.0.
reasoning.given: Dense 20-30B at long context removes MoE routing complexity and fits commodity 24GB VRAM, collapsing on-prem deployment cost.
reasoning.so_that: Mistral / Meta / DeepSeek / Gemma converge on a 20-30B Dense × 1M context recipe and 70B+ MoE retreats to multimodal / tool / long-horizon niches.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; ≥3 vendor 20-30B Dense releases + RTX 4090 / M4 Max as default on-prem VRAM.
reasoning.plain_language: A 27B model now matches a 400B one for coding and runs on one gaming GPU. Other labs will copy this recipe in months.

predictions.summary: Qwen3.6-27B Dense reaches flagship-grade coding (SWE-bench Verified 77.2%) on a single RTX 4090 with 1M context under Apache 2.0, beating its 397B MoE predecessor. By Q3 2026, Mistral, Meta, DeepSeek, and Gemma are expected to ship comparable 20-30B Dense × 1M context models, shifting on-prem VRAM defaults to RTX 4090 / M4 Max.

predictions.title: AI agent security standards extend to the "third-party-vendor boundary" and "URL-naming entropy.
predictions.body: **AI agent security standards extend to the "third-party-vendor boundary" and "URL-naming entropy."** The Mythos unauthorized-access incident (contractor + naming-convention guess + third-party leaked data) replays the Vercel-Context.ai supply-chain pattern, this time at a frontier-model access-control layer. By Q3 2026, "frontier model preview / early access third-party vendor environments" will be required to clear a common security baseline of SOC 2 Type II + FedRAMP Moderate + Frontier Model Forum, and endpoint URLs / subdomains will move to randomized, hard-to-guess naming. In plain language: In plain language: In plain language:

reasoning.because: The Mythos Preview breach combined contractor credentials, the Mercor leak, and naming-convention guessing — exactly replaying the Vercel-Context.ai supply-chain shape.
reasoning.given: Frontier-model preview environments rely on third-party vendors and predictable subdomains, so identity + naming entropy is the new attack surface.
reasoning.so_that: Third-party preview vendors must clear a SOC 2 Type II + FedRAMP Moderate + Frontier Model Forum baseline and endpoint URLs move to randomized naming.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; common third-party-vendor security baseline + randomized endpoint naming visible at frontier labs.
reasoning.plain_language: Hackers reached Anthropic's secret model by guessing its web address and using a contractor's stolen login. Soon labs will randomize URLs and require stricter contractor checks.

predictions.summary: The Mythos Preview unauthorized-access incident — contractor credentials, the Mercor leak, and a guessed naming convention — replays the Vercel-Context.ai supply-chain pattern at a frontier-model access-control layer. By Q3 2026, third-party preview vendors are expected to clear a SOC 2 Type II + FedRAMP Moderate + Frontier Model Forum baseline and endpoint URLs to move to randomized naming.
